stances.claim: The board of judges led by CJ Sereno was so impressed during candidate evaluation that it expanded the planned cohort from 10 to 11 scholars â€” and the FLP happily agreed â€” demonstrating that the screening process was substantive rather than mechanical, with merit overriding administrative target.
stances.rhetorical_move: expansion-as-quality-signal (one-extra-scholar as evidence of substantive screening)

stances.claim: FLP's philosophy â€” that 'justice and jobs, freedom and food, ethics and economics, democracy and development, nay, liberty and prosperity must always go together; one is useless without the other' â€” requires future lawyers to understand 'the intertwining relationship of law and business, and of regulation and entrepreneurship,' making the LibPros scholarship a vehicle for producing dual-fluency advocates.
stances.rhetorical_move: philosophy-statement via five-dyad enumeration with binary-inseparability framing

stances.claim: FLP's three companion activities â€” LibPros scholarships (school-specific), Ayala-cosponsored dissertation contest (nationwide), and 10 professorial chairs (with Metrobank Foundation + MPIC cosponsorship, 9 already filled) â€” represent a layered institutional strategy for embedding the liberty-and-prosperity philosophy at student, dissertation, and faculty levels simultaneously.
stances.rhetorical_move: three-layer-strategy framing (student + dissertation + faculty all addressed)

notable_anecdotes.summary: The 10-to-11 expansion: 'Only 10 were to be chosen. But after a thorough evaluation of the candidates, the board of judges, led by Chief Justice Maria Lourdes P. A. Sereno, was so impressed that it recommended 11. The Foundation for Liberty and Prosperity (FLP) happily agreed' â€” the column's opening anecdote and immediately the highest-credibility marker for the program's rigor.
The full named scholar roster with schools â€” the most extensive single named list in any FLP column, making the column itself the public record of the first LibPros cohort.
FLP's founding origin: 'organized in December 2011 during my 75th birthday' â€” making the foundation date personally rather than institutionally anchored.

The 'last Sunday's column' clarification on US electoral mechanics that closes the piece â€” using the postscript convention to address reader confusion from the prior week's column (E85 'Will Trump emulate Gore?') without losing the column's main D-series focus.

The FLP head-office sponsor list (First Philippine Holdings, Lopez Holdings, Meralco, PLDT, MPIC, San Miguel's Ramon S. Ang) â€” quietly cataloguing the corporate network underwriting FLP's physical operations.

one_paragraph_summary: Panganiban announces the first batch of LibPros law scholars â€” originally planned at 10 but expanded to 11 after the board of judges, led by CJ Maria Lourdes P. A. Sereno, was so impressed it recommended an extra scholar; the FLP happily agreed. The Dec 1, 2016 awarding ceremony at the new UP campus in Fort Bonifacio, Taguig will feature CJ Sereno as guest speaker; each scholar receives full scholarship (tuition + books + monthly stipend up to P200,000), and 10 new scholars will be selected every school year. The distribution across schools: Ateneo, FEU-Makati, San Beda, and UST 2 each; PUP, USC, UP 1 each â€” named: Sean James Borja, Kaycelle Anne Castillo, Ervin Frederick Dy, Rexlyn Ann Evora, Ken Kevin Ganchero, Jose Noel Hilario, Violeta Najarro Jr., Dion Ceasar Pascua, Ma Janine Pedernal, Tess Marie Tan, and Vanessa Gloria Vergara. The program is cosponsored by the Tan Yan Kee Foundation (TYKF) in cooperation with the Philippine Association of Law Schools (PALS); the board of judges includes CJ Sereno, FLP president Evelyn T. Dumdum, former education secretary Edilberto C. de Jesus, TYKF legal counsel Ma Cecilia L. Pesayco, and PALS president Ma Soledad D. Mawis, with FLP executive director Martin Angelo L. Esguerra assisting. Qualification requires being a third-year law student in an eligible school, top 10% of class, â‰¥83% (2.25) average across four prior semesters, full loads with no drops, dean's commendation, an essay on liberty and prosperity, and board screening. The FLP was organized in December 2011 during CJ's 75th birthday to perpetuate the legal philosophy that 'justice and jobs, freedom and food, ethics and economics, democracy and development, nay, liberty and prosperity must always go together; one is useless without the other.' Beyond the scholarships, FLP and the Ayala Group cosponsor a dissertation contest open to all law students nationwide (P300K champion, P200K runner-up, P100K each for 3 honorable mentions, P20K each for 20 qualifying entries); with Metrobank Foundation and MPIC, FLP has created 10 professorial chairs â€” 9 already filled by the law deans of Ateneo, FEU, USC, UP, UST, DLSU, Silliman, U Cordilleras, plus the PHILJA chancellor; USC dean Joan Largo will deliver her fourth professorial lecture in Cebu City on Dec 9. FLP acquired its Makati head office with assistance from First Philippine Holdings, Lopez Holdings, Meralco, PLDT, MPIC, and San Miguel's Ramon S. Ang. A postscript clarifies last Sunday's column on US elector selection: each state determines elector election methods by local law â€” in some, elector names are on the ballot; in others, presidential candidate names appear but votes still count for the electors, not the candidates directly.
